text: The surgeon begins the dissection by dividing the gastric column omentum to mobilize the greater curvature of the stomach, providing access to the lesser sac. Concurrently, lymphadenectomy of stations IV-D and IV-SB is performed.
The surgeon begins a clockwise dissection using an ultrasonic device to perform lymphadenectomy of station six, navigating through the abundant fat tissue in the region.

The surgeon locates and dissects the gastric epiploid vein, which is clipped and divided. This step is visually challenging, especially in obese patients, due to the complexity of accessing the gastric block vessels.
The surgeon dissects the gastric epipelagic artery, with the surgical field showing proximity to the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon positions a gauze underneath the duodenum to stabilize the area, then proceeds to dissect the gastroduodenal artery. The dissection continues towards the hepatic hilum, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy of station XII-A, isolates and divides the right gastric artery, and continues with lymphadenectomy of station V and station VIII-A.
The surgeon dissects the left gastric artery, progressing through the surgical field without performing dissection at stations seven and nine.

The surgeon clips and divides the short gastric vessels, followed by performing a lymphadenectomy at station one. Station two is removed, and a level IV-SA lymphadenectomy is conducted, with attention to stations eleven D and eleven P.
The surgeon staples the duodenum using a blue cartridge stapler, followed by stapling the esophagus with an articulated vise.
The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y reconstruction, utilizing the Orville device for the anastomosis. A 21 circular stapler is used to create the esophagojejunal anastomosis.
The surgeon reinforces the staple line using a 3-0 Prolene suture, securing the jejunal segment to the adjacent tissue.
The surgeon performs entero-anastomosis using a stapler, followed by closure of mesenteric defects. The adenostomy is completed, and the specimen is extracted through an extended optical port.
